summary: Joe Reis reflects on the 2.5-year journey since publishing 'Fundamentals of Data Engineering,' discussing how the book emerged from frustration with tool-focused learning and became a bestseller by teaching data engineering from first principles. He shares the intense personal challenges of writing—including working through COVID in a basement during a house remodel—and how the book transformed his career while attracting both devoted readers and inevitable critics.
key_points: The book was born from frustration that no one had written a tool-agnostic, first-principles guide to data engineering
Writing forces deep humility—there's a vast difference between doing work and explaining it from first principles for a general audience
Making things simple is harder than writing expert-level content because you must consider countless reader perspectives
Writing a book genuinely changes your career trajectory and confers 'authority' status
The data engineering lifecycle framework has been widely adopted in universities, businesses, and the industry
money_quotes: If you want to feel humbled, write a book. You start off feeling like you know your topic and quickly realize you're in for a harsh lesson in humility.

The hard part about writing a fundamentals book is distilling complexity into something simple and understandable for newcomers. I'd say it's more challenging than writing an expert-level book precisely because you're writing for a broad audience from countless perspectives.
There's your career before and after writing a book. This is very apt. If you want to elevate your profile, write a book. For whatever reason, writing a book puts you in a different class.
Having met countless data engineering practitioners of all levels worldwide, I've yet to meet even expert practitioners who have a complete grasp of all fundamental concepts in data engineering.
At one point, everyone starts at zero. Some move on from zero. Everyone is somewhere on their learning journey. Respect that.
key_insight: Writing a fundamentals book is harder than writing an expert-level one because distilling complexity into accessible knowledge for a broad audience requires both deep expertise and radical empathy for learners at every stage.
